title: A PLEA FOR A REFUGE IN WARREN–BLACKWOOD
summary: In a grievance to the Minister for Prevention of Family and Domestic Violence, Bevan said there is no refuge anywhere in Warren–Blackwood, in Manjimup, Bridgetown, Boyup Brook, Augusta, Margaret River or Denmark, and that the absence is a real-world safety failure. He told the house about a young Manjimup mother, whom he called Eve, who survived sustained and serious violence and repeated breaches of a restraining order, and who, with no local refuge, was forced to drive almost two hours with her three children to reach safety. He said the Bunbury refuge turned away 817 women and children last year for lack of capacity, and the Busselton refuge turned away 286 unassisted inquiries between January and June, 29 per cent of them women with three or more children, while local police are left to carry the strain of transporting victims hours away. He tabled a letter of support from the WA Police Union and called on the minister and the Premier to act now. Support is available: 1800RESPECT on 1800 737 732, or 000 in an emergency.
quote: Violence does not pick post codes, and neither should safety.
outcome: Delivered as a grievance to the Minister for Prevention of Family and Domestic Violence, putting the absence of any refuge in Warren–Blackwood, and one survivor's experience, on the parliamentary record with a tabled letter of support from the WA Police Union.

title: LEFT OUT OF THE FUNDING ROUND AGAIN
summary: In the same motion, Bevan said Margaret River Hospital had again been left out of the latest round of state infrastructure funding, despite longstanding calls to upgrade its emergency and maternity services. He said hospitals across Warren–Blackwood face staffing shortages and ageing infrastructure as the regional population ages and tourist numbers grow, and that telling people to drive to Bunbury, an hour and a half each way, is not a regional health strategy. He contrasted this with the hundreds of millions of dollars directed to metropolitan projects such as the Perth park and the new Swan River ferry terminals.
quote: When it comes to growing regional health care, the answer cannot simply be 'Go to Bunbury.'
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution supporting the motion condemning the Labor government for failing to deliver fundamental infrastructure and services. He used it to put practical state measures on aged care and the exclusion of Margaret River Hospital from the latest funding round on the parliamentary record.

title: PRACTICAL STATE STEPS ON AGED CARE
summary: In a motion condemning the government over infrastructure and service delivery, Bevan said that while aged care is primarily a federal responsibility, the state cannot simply throw up its hands. He set out practical measures the state could take: providing respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) vaccines to aged-care residents to prevent avoidable hospitalisations; offering payroll tax relief to aged-care providers delivering Commonwealth-funded services to improve their sustainability; investing in regional worker housing to attract aged-care and health professionals to towns like Manjimup, Bridgetown and Denmark; and expanding transport services so aged-care residents can reach medical appointments in regional hubs. He said the shortage of accessible aged-care beds and home-care packages is putting pressure on families, hospitals and the dignity of older Western Australians.
quote: Aged care is primarily a federal responsibility, but the state government cannot simply throw up its hands.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution supporting the motion condemning the Labor government for failing to deliver fundamental infrastructure and services. He used it to put practical state measures on aged care and the exclusion of Margaret River Hospital from the latest funding round on the parliamentary record.

title: A HERITAGE POOL SLIDING INTO DISREPAIR
summary: Bevan pointed to the historic natural swimming pool at Pemberton, a heritage-listed site that is at once a marquee tourist attraction and a community asset, but in significant decline, with the dam wall, ablutions, fencing, landscaping, signage, accessibility and shade all needing work. He said a private business is interested in partnering on the site but cannot proceed while the basic infrastructure is missing, and that because the land is crown land managed by the Pemberton Visitor Centre rather than the shire, applying for government funding is difficult. He said it is a valuable community and tourism asset sliding into disrepair because no level of government is taking the lead.
quote: It is slowly sliding into disrepair because no level of government is taking the lead.
outcome: A contribution to the second reading debate on the Sports and Entertainment Trust Bill 2025, using grassroots facilities across Warren–Blackwood to argue that regional sporting infrastructure is being left behind.

title: FORCED INTO DEBT TO FINISH A REC CENTRE
summary: Speaking on the Sports and Entertainment Trust Bill 2025, Bevan said the gap in grassroots sporting infrastructure has real consequences for small towns, and gave the Boyup Brook recreation and evacuation centre as the example. The $5.2 million facility drew an $800,000 federal emergency-management grant on the condition the shire matched it dollar for dollar, which it did, with the local community raising $70,000 and the football association $50,000. When the shire sought $3 million for the shortfall from the Department of Local Government, Sport and Cultural Industries, it was approved only $1.5 million, leaving the council to take out a $2 million loan or lose the project. He said underinvestment in regional sport drives down participation, leaks spending to larger towns and burns out the volunteers who hold clubs together, and noted Manjimup's Rea Park redevelopment is similarly reliant on the state delivering its promised funding for later stages.
quote: When a local council has to go into debt just to finish what should be a jointly funded community project, we have a broken system.
outcome: A contribution to the second reading debate on the Sports and Entertainment Trust Bill 2025, using grassroots facilities across Warren–Blackwood to argue that regional sporting infrastructure is being left behind.

title: NOT ONE REFUGE, AND NONE PROMISED
summary: During the 16 Days in WA campaign, Bevan asked the Premier how the government could claim to be strengthening support for victim-survivors when there is not a single family and domestic violence refuge anywhere in Warren–Blackwood, one of the largest regional electorates in the state, forcing local families to rely on overstretched services hundreds of kilometres away. He noted the government had announced expanded refuges in Albany, Broome and Midland, but none in his electorate. In a supplementary he asked the Premier to commit to funding and delivering a dedicated women's refuge in Warren–Blackwood within the next 12 months. Support is available: 1800RESPECT on 1800 737 732, or 000 in an emergency.
quote: Will the Premier commit to funding and delivering a dedicated women's refuge in Warren–Blackwood within the next 12 months?
outcome: The Premier, Roger Cook, pointed to more than $700 million invested in family and domestic violence initiatives since 2017, a 41 per cent lift in refuge capacity, 91 new units with 102 more in the pipeline, and recent openings in Albany, Kalgoorlie and Broome, but did not commit to a refuge in Warren–Blackwood.

title: NO STAFF HOUSING, NO AGED CARE
summary: Bevan said the regional housing shortage is now a direct threat to aged care. He said many regional aged-care and home-care providers are struggling commercially because staff cannot be attracted or retained, in part because of the housing and infrastructure constraints, and that when aged-care workers cannot be housed, service availability for older regional residents is put at risk. He called for regional aged-care and home-care workforce housing to be included in the Government Regional Officers' Housing program, and for housing grants such as the Regional Housing Support Fund to be adjusted for the infrastructure constraints of smaller shires.
quote: Failing to house aged-care workers is a direct threat to service availability and older regional Australians.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution to the motion on the failure to provide adequate Government Regional Officers' Housing in regional communities. He used it to put the worker-housing shortage, the erosion of local government revenue, and the threat to regional aged care on the parliamentary record.

title: $150,000 IN RATES REROUTED TO TREASURY
summary: Bevan said state decisions are stripping small shires of the revenue they need to fill the gap. He said the Shire of Boyup Brook is set to lose around $150,000 in rates because the Forest Products Commission will pay equivalent amounts to Treasury rather than directly to the shire under the Forest Products Act 2000, and that the shire was advised only a couple of weeks earlier, after the budget was adopted, even though tree farms and commercial plantations add wear to local roads and infrastructure. He added that the Local Government Amendment (Rating of Certain Mining Licences) Bill 2025 would further reduce the shire's capacity, removing about $29,320 a year from rates on its 24 miscellaneous mining licence properties. He called on the government to revisit the Forest Products Commission decision and ensure shires are compensated for the road and infrastructure impacts.
quote: The decision to allow FPC to pay equivalent rates to Treasury rather than to local shires breaks the trust local government placed in government to maintain equitable funding.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution to the motion on the failure to provide adequate Government Regional Officers' Housing in regional communities. He used it to put the worker-housing shortage, the erosion of local government revenue, and the threat to regional aged care on the parliamentary record.

title: NO HOUSE FOR THE WORKERS TOWNS NEED
summary: Speaking to the motion on Government Regional Officers' Housing, Bevan said a shortage of adequate, affordable GROH stock is making it hard for regional towns to attract and keep police, teachers, nurses and other key public servants, with many leaving or never coming. He used the Shire of Boyup Brook as the example: the shire has had to lease houses on the open market to accommodate staff the state should house, including the local police officer, and runs the local medical practice with the doctor also working at the state hospital, yet receives no state assistance to operate the practice and still leases a house to support staffing. He called for an immediate audit of GROH stock across regional WA and a clear timeline to deliver new housing in towns like Boyup Brook, Bridgetown, Nannup and Denmark.
quote: The government's push for people to move to the regions rings hollow when housing and water infrastructure is missing.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution to the motion on the failure to provide adequate Government Regional Officers' Housing in regional communities. He used it to put the worker-housing shortage, the erosion of local government revenue, and the threat to regional aged care on the parliamentary record.

title: A DECADE OF SHIRE RATES, LEGISLATED AWAY
summary: Speaking on the Local Government Amendment (Rating of Certain Mining Licences) Bill 2025, Bevan said the bill would retrospectively exempt certain mining tenements and miscellaneous and small prospecting licences on crown land from local government rates, following a Supreme Court ruling, extinguishing potential rate revenue from 1 July 2017 to 30 June 2026. He said small shires rely on even modest mining activity, the miscellaneous licences for access roads, water pipelines and transport corridors that place real demands on local roads, waste services and community facilities, and that councils would have to refund payments already received, could not reissue rate notices for the period, and were offered no compensation. He warned that retrospective legislation undermines trust and local planning and sets a precedent, asked whether Treasury had modelled the cost to councils and whether the WA Local Government Association was consulted, and called for a full financial impact statement, consultation with affected shires and targeted compensation. The bill concerns licences including those around the 130-year-old Talison lithium mine at Greenbushes in his electorate, which produces more than 20 per cent of the world's lithium.
quote: This is not about protecting local governments from legal confusion; it is about extinguishing nearly a decade's worth of revenue.
outcome: A contribution to the second reading debate on the Local Government Amendment (Rating of Certain Mining Licences) Bill 2025, supporting the member for Geraldton's amendments and putting on the record his call for Treasury modelling, consultation with affected shires and the WA Local Government Association, and targeted compensation before the bill proceeds.

title: THE NUMBERS AT A 30-YEAR HIGH
summary: Bevan set out the scale of the problem. He told the house that in 2024 the number of assault victims in WA rose 10 per cent to 47,045, the highest in 30 years, with two-thirds, more than 30,000 cases, family and domestic violence related; that sexual assault victims rose 9 per cent to 3,721, a 32-year high; and that of 55 homicide victims, 40 per cent, 22 people, died as a result of family and domestic violence. He said homelessness services are overwhelmed, with more than 28,000 requests for support unmet in 2024, up 39 per cent since 2017, and that breaches of family and domestic violence orders rose from 4,005 to 4,796 in a single year while the number of offenders proceeded against rose 26 per cent. He said the protections that already exist must be enforced. Support is available: 1800RESPECT on 1800 737 732, or 000 in an emergency.
quote: In 2024, the number of assault victims in WA increased by 10% to 47,045, the highest in 30 years.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution supporting the motion condemning the WA Labor government over community safety. He used it to put the absence of family and domestic violence support across Warren–Blackwood, and the strain on regional police, on the parliamentary record.

title: POLICE CARRYING A BROKEN SYSTEM
summary: Bevan said the absence of local refuges turns a safety problem into a policing one. With no local crisis accommodation, officers responding to a domestic violence call-out may take the victim to hospital, then drive to a shelter in Busselton or Bunbury, or book a motel if there is no space, a round trip that can run to seven hours and end beyond their shift, leaving the town with reduced police presence and neighbouring stations covering. He said the strain runs through the justice system too, with the Department of Justice using auxiliary officers for regional court security, protective services officers backfilling, and general duties officers backfilling again, leaving fewer police on the front line doing work they were not recruited for. Support is available: 1800RESPECT on 1800 737 732, or 000 in an emergency.
quote: Police are being asked to carry the weight of social failures far beyond their remit.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution supporting the motion condemning the WA Labor government over community safety. He used it to put the absence of family and domestic violence support across Warren–Blackwood, and the strain on regional police, on the parliamentary record.

title: NOT ONE WOMEN'S REFUGE IN THE ELECTORATE
summary: Speaking to the motion on community safety, Bevan said there is not a single women's refuge anywhere in Warren–Blackwood, not in Manjimup, Bridgetown, Denmark, Augusta or Margaret River, with the closest options in Albany, Bunbury or Busselton if there is space. He said outreach support is thin, stretched and delivered from outside the electorate, leaving no town-based support at all. He told the house that women have said they stayed in violent homes because they had nowhere to go, and that others do not call for help knowing local police are already stretched. He called for a refuge in Warren–Blackwood, probably two so they are not hours apart, town-based outreach in Bridgetown, Nannup and Walpole, and investment in prevention and early intervention. Support is available: 1800RESPECT on 1800 737 732, or 000 in an emergency.
quote: In my electorate of Warren–Blackwood, there is not a single woman's refuge.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution supporting the motion condemning the WA Labor government over community safety. He used it to put the absence of family and domestic violence support across Warren–Blackwood, and the strain on regional police, on the parliamentary record.

title: TWO MONTHS FOR A GENERIC REPLY
summary: Bevan said Witchcliffe has no mains water, leaving many residents to buy water to fill their rainwater tanks through summer. On 19 August he requested, through the minister's office, a formal briefing from the Water Corporation. He said the reply came two months later, on 17 October, and was generic, with no details, no plan and no acknowledgement of the briefing request. He said communities deserve clarity rather than copies of the strategic asset plan.
quote: That is not engagement; it is delay and deflection.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution supporting the motion condemning the Cook Labor government's management of Western Australia's water infrastructure, service and supply. He used it to put the stalled regional water projects across Warren–Blackwood on the parliamentary record and to call for a targeted, transparent, regionally focused infrastructure funding plan.

title: 126 LOTS SOLD, THE PROJECT STALLED
summary: In Pemberton, Bevan said a developer has approval for 126 residential lots already taken up by families and workers, but the project is stalled because the Water Corporation wants $7 million for a treatment plant upgrade, despite the developer already contributing $1 million in standard charges. He said the project could deliver more than $75 million in housing and construction, unlock $30 million in capital works in the next stage and support more than 300 lots over time. He also pointed out that mains sewerage is being rolled out to the new part of town but not the old part, even though the same pipe runs past both, which he called poor delivery rather than good planning.
quote: The same pipe goes right past both. The same need exists on both sides of town, but only half the town has been connected.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution supporting the motion condemning the Cook Labor government's management of Western Australia's water infrastructure, service and supply. He used it to put the stalled regional water projects across Warren–Blackwood on the parliamentary record and to call for a targeted, transparent, regionally focused infrastructure funding plan.

title: A 360-LOT TOWN STALLED BY A WATER BILL
summary: Bevan said the Shire of Boyup Brook has a developer ready for a 360-lot residential subdivision, demand from retirees, young families and downsizers, but a $7 million water infrastructure bill has stopped it. He said the Water Corporation refused to help and told the shire to look elsewhere, though there is no elsewhere, and that infill housing is blocked because the town has no deep sewerage, so lots under 1,000 square metres cannot be subdivided under state planning policies, with the Water Corporation confirming no current or future plans to deliver it. He said even a modest $2 million pipeline to connect two existing dams, which would boost drought resilience and provide emergency supply, has not been funded.
quote: The town needs water and it needs leadership from the state government.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution supporting the motion condemning the Cook Labor government's management of Western Australia's water infrastructure, service and supply. He used it to put the stalled regional water projects across Warren–Blackwood on the parliamentary record and to call for a targeted, transparent, regionally focused infrastructure funding plan.

title: A METRE OF RAIN A YEAR, NO PERMANENT WATER
summary: Bevan told the house Northcliffe sits in one of the highest-rainfall areas in the state, consistently more than 1,000 millimetres a year, yet has no permanent water supply. Instead water is carted in by truck every day, all year, from Pemberton or Manjimup, a round trip of between 60 and 120 kilometres. He said a dam would give the town a reliable supply and improve its safety against bushfire, and would likely have been more economical than a decade of trucking, and that a rainwater-tank subsidy would be a simple quick fix for community safety and self-sufficiency. He said the lack of water infrastructure is holding back a town well suited to residential growth.
quote: That is one metre of rainfall every year, yet there is no permanent water supply.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution supporting the motion condemning the Cook Labor government's management of Western Australia's water infrastructure, service and supply. He used it to put the stalled regional water projects across Warren–Blackwood on the parliamentary record and to call for a targeted, transparent, regionally focused infrastructure funding plan.

title: A RESCUE ON THE BLACKWOOD RIVER
summary: In a member's statement, Bevan honoured a Blackwood River rescue at Bridgetown on 5 August. When a young woman was swept down the fast-flowing river, Thomas Bolas, a 77-year-old groundsman at Ford House, raised the alarm, ran along the bank to track her, then swam in and kept her afloat until help arrived. Senior Constable Mike Smith, newly on the scene, handed his keys to a bystander, entered the water and kept the woman stable until emergency crews reached them. Both men received certificates from the Commissioner of Police and bravery awards from Royal Life Saving Western Australia. Bevan used the statement to thank them and to back regional emergency services and police with the resources and respect he said their work deserves.
quote: In communities like ours in Warren–Blackwood, police officers are not just uniformed personnel; they are coaches, neighbours, first responders and friends.
outcome: Delivered as a member's statement honouring a Bridgetown river rescue on 5 August and the regional emergency services and police behind it.

title: STAGE 2 IS SHOVEL-READY, STILL NO YES
summary: Returning to Margaret River Senior High School a month after first raising it in this debate, Bevan said the stage 2 master plan is finished and the project is shovel-ready, with the only thing missing a yes from the government. He said the school, built for 650, now holds more than 1,240 students, well past the 1,200 capacity delivered by the 2016–17 stage 1 upgrade, and that the government's answer remains two transportables. He noted the Leader of the Opposition had visited both Margaret River and Manjimup Senior High Schools within seven months of taking the role, while neither the Premier nor the Minister for Education had set foot in either despite repeated invitations, and that Manjimup Senior High School now sits at number 2 on the state's school defect list. He said the same pattern of neglect runs through Nannup, Greenbushes, Bridgetown, Denmark and Boyup Brook.
quote: You will not find a more shovel-ready project than Margaret River Senior High School.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution supporting the motion on the government's neglect of school infrastructure, moved by the member for Carine. He used it to press that the Margaret River Senior High School stage 2 plans are complete and waiting only on funding.

title: $2 MILLION TO GUARD EMPTY LAND
summary: Bevan asked the Minister for Health Infrastructure to release the old Swan District Hospital site for an aged-care facility, noting the government has spent more than $2 million on private security guarding the vacant site while the state faces a clear shortage of aged-care places. In a supplementary, with the government asking doctors and nurses to cut costs, he asked for the total amount spent to date on private security at the site.
quote: With a clear shortage of aged-care places, will the minister now release the land for an aged-care facility?
outcome: The minister, John Carey, said the land is part of the South West Native Title Settlement process. The supplementary asking for the total security spend was ruled not directly related to the first question, and when offered the chance to respond the minister declined.

title: TRANSPORTABLES ARE NOT A BUILDING PLAN
summary: Bevan said the government's growing use of transportable classrooms is being treated as permanent infrastructure rather than a temporary measure, and does nothing to fix deep-seated safety problems or future-proof schools. He said regional students are being treated as second-class citizens while billions go to metropolitan schools and growth suburbs, and acknowledged the principals, teachers and P&C volunteers carrying a load that should be the government's. He called on the government to have the Minister for Education tour the schools, immediately fund stage 2 at Margaret River Senior High School, allocate urgent capital works to Greenbushes, Nannup, Bridgetown, Denmark and Boyup Brook, conduct an independent audit of transportable classroom use across WA and publish the findings, and commit to a school infrastructure program that gives equal weight to regional needs.
quote: We cannot accept a two-tiered education system in which city students get new buildings and country students get excuses.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution to the Opposition motion condemning the Cook Labor government for neglecting Western Australia's school infrastructure and its growing reliance on transportable classrooms. He used it to put the condition of Warren–Blackwood's schools on the parliamentary record.

title: FIVE SCHOOLS MAKING DO WITH LESS
summary: Beyond Margaret River, Bevan catalogued the condition of schools across Warren–Blackwood. At Greenbushes Primary School he said pine batons have been screwed to the ceilings of four or five classrooms to stop them collapsing on students, with ongoing problems in the floors, ceilings and outdated plumbing, and a library still housed in the old police station. He said Nannup's basketball courts, toilets and home economics room remain in limbo; Bridgetown High School has lost more than half a million dollars in student-centred funding since 2022 and relies on donations to run a breakfast club and replace toilets; Denmark Senior High School has had mould confirmed in 2022 and again in July 2025; and Boyup Brook District High School is in poor condition through years of deferred maintenance. He asked the Minister for Education to visit Boyup Brook to see the conditions firsthand.
quote: Why should our kids have to learn in conditions that would never be accepted in Perth?
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution to the Opposition motion condemning the Cook Labor government for neglecting Western Australia's school infrastructure and its growing reliance on transportable classrooms. He used it to put the condition of Warren–Blackwood's schools on the parliamentary record.

title: ALREADY OVER CAPACITY, STILL NO STAGE 2
summary: Bevan told the house Margaret River Senior High School, originally built for 650 students, will pass 1,240 enrolments within six months, even though its stage 1 upgrade lifted capacity only to 1,200 and 1,220 students already attend. He said the government's offer of two transportables and a combined woodwork and metalwork room does not resolve the school's storage, machinery-safety and student-movement problems, and that staff have reconfigured classrooms, used corridors for storage, given up a staffroom and built makeshift welding bays out of the school's own budget. With enrolments projected to reach 2,000, he said stage 2, costed at $40 million to $50 million, remains unfunded, and that the Minister for Education had advised she was not available to visit for the rest of the year. He said some families are now relocating to Perth when their children reach high school age because the local facilities cannot keep up.
quote: No family should be forced to leave a community in a town the size of Margaret River just to secure a decent education for their kids.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution to the Opposition motion condemning the Cook Labor government for neglecting Western Australia's school infrastructure and its growing reliance on transportable classrooms. He used it to put the condition of Warren–Blackwood's schools on the parliamentary record.

title: WILL THE PRINCIPAL CHAPLAINCY PROGRAM CONTINUE?
summary: Bevan asked the Minister for Education why she would not guarantee ongoing support for the principal chaplaincy program in Warren–Blackwood, which he said is the only source of face-to-face pastoral care and wellbeing support for many regional principals. In a supplementary he asked whether she would meet Warren–Blackwood principals this month to hear their concerns before the program's future is decided.
quote: Will the minister meet with the Warren–Blackwood principals this month to hear their concerns before finalising the program's future?
outcome: The minister said she was very willing to support principals and school leaders across the state and pointed to having met several hundred school leaders since March, including at a recent primary principals conference. She did not guarantee the chaplaincy program's future or commit to meeting Warren–Blackwood principals this month.

title: WASTE IS NOT CONSERVATION
summary: Speaking on the Reserves Bill 2025, which redraws reserve boundaries, updates classifications and carries forward elements of earlier forest management plans, Bevan said the bill is silent on what happens to the timber removed during ecological thinning. He said thinning is necessary for forest health and fire risk, but its by-product is often high-quality jarrah and marri that should go to furniture, building and craft use rather than being chipped, burnt or sold as firewood. He pointed to Manjimup, Nannup and Bridgetown, where local builders and furniture makers are short of quality hardwood, and called for a transparent register of thinning operations, timber graded before removal, and local processors given first right of refusal. He also warned that locking up more forest without extra funding to the Department of Biodiversity, Conservation and Attractions for fire mitigation, biodiversity monitoring and weed and pest control risks turning reserves into neglected liabilities.
quote: Logs suitable for milling should never be chipped, burnt or sold as firewood.
outcome: A contribution to the second reading debate on the Reserves Bill 2025, putting on the record his call for a transparent framework so that timber from ecological thinning is graded and directed to its highest-value use, and three questions to the minister: whether timber removed under the bill will be available for commercial processing, whether high-grade logs will be guaranteed against waste, and whether local communities will have input into how it is managed.

title: WILL REGIONAL SENIORS GET PRIORITY FOR CARE PACKAGES?
summary: Bevan asked the Minister for Aged Care, Simone McGurk, to stand behind her claim, reported in The West Australian, that she would seek nearly half of the 20,000 home care packages the federal government has promised to fast-track. He asked what confidence seniors could have that the packages would be secured, and whether she would guarantee that any extra packages allocated to WA would be prioritised for the regional communities most in need. In a supplementary he asked her to table any correspondence her office had had with the federal Minister for Aged Care since the announcement.
quote: Will the minister guarantee that the additional home care packages allocated to WA will be prioritised for the regional communities most in need?
outcome: The minister said the packages are funded and regulated by the federal government and were not within her control, that she would advocate for at least WA's population share of 10.2 per cent, and pressed the opposition on what it was doing to lobby its federal colleagues. She did not guarantee regional prioritisation. On the correspondence she said she had no objection to making it public but pointed Bevan to the questions on notice process rather than committing to table it.

title: PINE PLANTATIONS PRICING OUT FARMERS
summary: Bevan said prime agricultural land is under pressure from wind farms and pine plantations, with the planning framework lacking safeguards and communities not meaningfully consulted. He said the Forest Products Commission is entering the market with deep pockets and offering well above market value to secure farmland for pine plantations, distorting the rural land market and pricing out the producers who keep the agricultural economy running. He said pine does not need to grow on prime farmland, pointing to the Gnangara plantation growing in sand.
quote: Farmers are finding it increasingly difficult to compete on land prices as the Forest Products Commission enters the market with deep pockets to secure farmland for pine plantations.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution to the Opposition motion condemning the Cook Labor government over agriculture and biosecurity. He used it to put the live sheep export transition, the shot-hole borer response and pressure on prime agricultural land on the parliamentary record.

title: THE BORER ERADICATION RACE WAS LOST
summary: Bevan said the polyphagous shot-hole borer, first detected in WA in August 2021 and a serious threat to horticulture and native vegetation including Warren–Blackwood's avocado plantations and native forests, can no longer be eradicated, with the national management group moving to containment. He said the $26.5 million now committed to management is money that could have gone to earlier and stronger eradication, and that the burden is increasingly falling on local government, industry and farmers.
quote: The national management group has now admitted that this pest can no longer be eradicated, and we have moved to containment.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution to the Opposition motion condemning the Cook Labor government over agriculture and biosecurity. He used it to put the live sheep export transition, the shot-hole borer response and pressure on prime agricultural land on the parliamentary record.

title: WEAK ADVOCACY ON LIVE SHEEP EXPORT
summary: Speaking to the Opposition's motion, Bevan said the state Minister for Agriculture and Food had offered little defence of the live sheep export industry ahead of the federal ban by sea from 1 May 2028, taking more than 140 days to even meet the federally appointed transition advocate. He said confidence in the industry is crumbling, flock numbers and wool sales are falling and young people are leaving, and argued the $139.7 million federal compensation package appears to favour processors and abattoirs over the farmers at the farm gate who carry the risk.
quote: The WA agriculture minister took over 140 days, or over four months, before she even met with a person tasked with helping Western Australia manage this transition.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution to the Opposition motion condemning the Cook Labor government over agriculture and biosecurity. He used it to put the live sheep export transition, the shot-hole borer response and pressure on prime agricultural land on the parliamentary record.

title: 200 ELDERLY STRANDED IN HOSPITAL BEDS
summary: Speaking to the Opposition's motion, Bevan said about 200 elderly Western Australians are stranded in public hospitals, medically cleared for discharge but with nowhere to go, and cited the Auditor General's finding that 486 patients spent a combined 40,000 unnecessary days in hospital at a cost of about $95 million, with regional patients waiting around 50 days against 11.4 in Perth. He told the story of a Manjimup family whose ACAT assessment wait blew out from three to over five months, after which the 88-year-old mother, having fractured her pelvis, could not be discharged for lack of an aged-care bed and was moved from Manjimup to Nannup, leaving her 91-year-old husband alone on the farm facing a 12-month home-care wait. He said community resource centres are running a local Staying in Place model on fumes, with little formal funding.
quote: Around 200 elderly Western Australians are stuck in public hospitals, medically cleared for discharge but unable to leave due to a lack of aged-care beds and home care support.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution to the motion on aged-care patients remaining in hospital, moved by the Leader of the Opposition. He used it to put the Auditor General's findings and a Manjimup family's experience on the parliamentary record.

title: THREE YEARS ON FROM THE AUDITOR GENERAL
summary: Bevan asked the Minister for Health why, nearly three years after the Auditor General's 2022 report Management of Long Stay Patients in Public Hospitals found WA had no effective system for managing long-stay patients, the government still pointed to aged-care patients as the cause of hospital pressure rather than fixing the system, and what specific action had been taken to move aged-care patients out of hospital in regional areas where services are reduced. In a supplementary he asked why the government keeps dragging its feet on aged care in hospitals.
quote: Why is it that nearly three years after the report, the Cook Labor government continues to shift blame onto aged-care patients for clogging our hospitals instead of fixing the system?
outcome: The Minister for Health, Meredith Hammat, said about 200 such patients are in the system at any time, peaking near 250 over winter, but described aged care as a federal responsibility and said she would travel to Canberra with the Premier to press for more beds and home-care packages. She pointed to the Time to Think program, a $30 million Transition Care Program and a loan scheme to boost aged-care beds, and rejected the suggestion the government was dragging its feet. She did not set out regional-specific actions.

title: THE FRONT OFFICE OF REGIONAL TOWNS
summary: In a member's statement, Bevan set out the role of the more than 100 community resource centres across regional WA, from the Kimberley to the Great Southern: locally governed not-for-profit hubs offering public computers, internet, printing, training and small-business support, and acting as visitor centres, event venues, volunteer hubs and food pantries. He said that in many smaller communities they are the front office for government services, helping residents reach Centrelink, Medicare, licensing, aged-care navigation and the Staying in Place program, and argued that increased investment in CRCs in next year's budget is not only justified but essential.
quote: In many remote and smaller communities, CRCs are the front office for government services.
outcome: Delivered as a member's statement, putting on the parliamentary record the role community resource centres play across regional WA and Bevan's call for increased investment in next year's budget.

title: AMBULANCE FUNDING FALLING AS DEMAND RISES
summary: Bevan said that despite rising demand in the regions, the forward estimates show ambulance and patient transport funding declining year on year. He said he had asked for the assumptions behind the decline and how service levels would be maintained, and had not received an answer.
quote: Despite regional demand increasing, the forward estimates show a decline in funding year on year.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution to the Opposition motion condemning the Cook Labor government over its handling of the health system. He used it to put the regional health infrastructure gaps in Warren–Blackwood on the parliamentary record.

title: WHERE ARE THE URGENT CARE CLINICS?
summary: Bevan said that in February the government promised in writing to deliver urgent care clinics across the South West, including Warren–Blackwood, to relieve pressure on emergency departments, but that with the 2025–26 budget handed down there was still no detail on which towns, what timeline or what funding.
quote: Where are the clinics? What is the timeline? Which towns? What funding? Still no answers.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution to the Opposition motion condemning the Cook Labor government over its handling of the health system. He used it to put the regional health infrastructure gaps in Warren–Blackwood on the parliamentary record.

title: EMERGENCY CARE UNDER A LEAKING ROOF
summary: Bevan said the emergency department roof at Bridgetown Hospital is leaking and patients are being treated under a roof that needs replacing. He put the re-roofing cost at about $1 million, less than 0.1 per cent of the government's $1 billion infrastructure program, and said there was no clear answer on whether it is funded, no line item and no transparency.
quote: At Bridgetown Hospital the situation is bordering on the absurd. The roof of the emergency department is leaking.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution to the Opposition motion condemning the Cook Labor government over its handling of the health system. He used it to put the regional health infrastructure gaps in Warren–Blackwood on the parliamentary record.

title: RELEASE THE BUSINESS CASE, FIX THE HOSPITAL
summary: Bevan said Margaret River Hospital, in a booming tourism town, is showing signs of neglect, and that the government had prepared a business case for upgrades but would not release it. He read from correspondence by local residents John and Di describing a nine-week stay: a patient-room light broken for eight weeks despite a work order, a recurring damp ceiling patched each winter rather than fixed, uneven disabled parking bays, call buttons that sometimes fail, carpeted wards raising hygiene concerns, no patient hoist available on arrival, and nights with one nurse covering ten patients. He noted the Premier had suggested people could drive to Bunbury Regional Hospital for care.
quote: The Labor government has developed a business case for hospital upgrades, but will not release it.
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution to the Opposition motion condemning the Cook Labor government over its handling of the health system. He used it to put the regional health infrastructure gaps in Warren–Blackwood on the parliamentary record.

title: STILL NO CT SCANNER AT MANJIMUP
summary: Bevan told the house Manjimup hospital, a regional hub for a large and ageing population, still has no CT scanner despite years of formal requests from local doctors, documented in correspondence and raised at budget estimates. He quoted a local rural GP who said that because patients must be transferred between hospitals for imaging, they cannot get timely scans, limiting treatment and reducing the chance of full recovery, and said stroke and trauma patients are missing the window for time-critical care while the scanner appears nowhere in the government's $1 billion health investment program.
quote: Manjimup doctors have asked and asked for a CT scanner for years. Where is it in the $1 billion investment program?
outcome: Part of Bevan's contribution to the Opposition motion condemning the Cook Labor government over its handling of the health system. He used it to put the regional health infrastructure gaps in Warren–Blackwood on the parliamentary record.

title: LOANS FOR 500 BEDS, BUT NOT YET
summary: Bevan asked how much is set aside for the aged-care bed loan scheme; the $100 million commitment was restated but no funding flows until 2026–27.
ask: Bevan asked how much funding is set aside for the low-interest loans offered to aged-care providers to deliver an estimated 500 new concessional aged-care beds, what consultation had occurred with providers, and whether allocation would be first-in, first-served.
answer: The minister said this is the $100 million election commitment to help providers deliver up to 500 additional concessional beds, including new builds, expansions, refurbishments and beds in areas of market failure such as parts of regional WA. She said the scheme is still being designed and consulted on, with funding expected to flow from 2026–27, that she would reinstate a provider advisory panel, and that allocation would be based on criteria and greatest need rather than first-in, first-served.
finding: The $100 million and the 500-bed target are restated from the election commitment, but the scheme is still being designed and no funding flows until 2026–27.

title: SUPPORT FOR OLDER PEOPLE WITH DISABILITY ENDING
summary: Bevan asked why disability support funding for older Australians ends after 2026–27 and how non-NDIS older people will cope; the answer was framed narrowly.
ask: Bevan asked why the government will cease all funding of disability support for older Australians after 2026–27, how older Western Australians with disability who are not eligible for the NDIS will keep accessing services, and what prevents service disruption for existing regional clients.
answer: The minister said the line item relates specifically to the Quadriplegic Centre at Shenton Park and the transition of its remaining long-stay residents over 65 to a new 20-unit high-care facility being built by Multiple Sclerosis WA, supported by state grants of $9 million and a further $5 million. She said regional older residents with disability needs remain a Commonwealth responsibility through the NDIS, with their health needs met by the WA Country Health Service.
finding: The minister framed the ending funding narrowly as the Quadriplegic Centre transition, and said older regional people with disability outside that program fall to the Commonwealth rather than the state.

title: VIRTUAL ED, BUT THE NUMBERS DEFERRED
summary: Bevan asked what the WA virtual emergency department expansion will cost and save; the funding and projections were referred to the Minister for Health.
ask: Bevan asked what funding is allocated to expanding the WA virtual emergency department (WAVED) in 2025–26 and the forward years, what the expansion involves and over what timeframe, and how many emergency department presentations it is expected to avoid and hospital beds free up.
answer: The minister said WAVED falls more firmly within the Minister for Health's portfolio and declined the funding and bed-projection detail, but the department explained the service gives people virtual access to emergency care, like telehealth, reached through 000 or Healthdirect, and is being extended so residential care facilities have direct access.
finding: The minister confirmed the virtual ED is being extended to residential aged care but referred the funding figures and the projected savings in presentations and beds to the Minister for Health.

title: PALLIATIVE CARE IN AGED CARE WOUND BACK
summary: Bevan asked why dedicated palliative care funding in aged care shows as discontinued, and how regional residents will keep receiving timely care.
ask: Bevan asked why funding for Comprehensive Palliative Care in Aged Care shows as discontinued in the 2025–26 budget, what is replacing it, and how regional aged-care residents will keep receiving timely palliative care.
answer: The minister said the National Partnership Agreement aims to improve end-of-life care in residential aged-care homes and that the Commonwealth had offered a two-year extension for 2024–26 worth $1.97 million, reflected in the budget line. On regional delivery, the head of WA Country Health Service said its palliative care program has more than doubled over recent years, runs close to 24 hours a day and supports more than 1,650 clients across community, hospital and residential settings.
finding: The dedicated line shows as discontinued; the minister pointed to a two-year federal extension and to country health palliative services to cover the gap.

title: ASSESSMENT FUNDING DROPS TO ZERO
summary: Bevan asked why aged-care assessment funding falls to zero across the forward estimates, and was told a 12-month contract would appear at the midyear review.
ask: Bevan asked why the Aged Care Assessment Program allocation falls from $10.2 million in 2023–24 to zero across the forward estimates, whether that reflects a transfer to the Commonwealth or a service cut, and what keeps assessments running in regional and remote areas where access is already limited.
answer: The minister said the state has delivered aged-care assessments under a 40-year partnership with the Commonwealth, about 20,000 a year, and that a 12-month contract had just been finalised and would appear in the midyear review. She said regional assessment services would continue without interruption, but noted the longer delays are often after assessment, in accessing Commonwealth-funded in-home or long-stay support.
finding: The assessment line reads as zero across the forward estimates; the minister said a 12-month contract has been struck and will be shown at the midyear review, leaving the ongoing funding to be confirmed there.

title: CARE HUBS FOR THE CITY FIRST
summary: In aged-care estimates, Bevan asked where the new older adult care hubs will be and whether the regions get one, and found regional access is conditional.
ask: Bevan asked where the three older adult community care hubs announced for 2025 and 2026 will be located, whether any will be in regional areas, and what services beyond virtual care will support regional ageing residents.
answer: The minister said the $24.3 million election commitment funds three older adult community-integrated care hubs based in the catchment areas of the three metropolitan health service providers, and that work for Western Australians outside the metropolitan area would follow only if the initial hubs are successful. She said much of the broader health detail falls to the Minister for Health.
finding: The first three care hubs will be metropolitan; regional access is conditional on the metropolitan hubs being judged a success first.

title: IS THE IR COMMISSION UNDER-RESOURCED?
summary: Bevan asked whether more funding would speed up the Industrial Relations Commission; the minister said no party had reported delays and none had been requested.
ask: Bevan asked whether a greater quantum of funding to the WA Industrial Relations Commission or the Industrial Magistrates Court would let matters be dealt with faster, and whether either body had requested more funding.
answer: The minister said she had not had feedback from any industrial party about delays or poor service on the time taken to resolve matters, that many applications go to conciliation under section 44 of the act, and that when she met the Chief Commissioner about six weeks earlier he had not requested additional resources for his team.
finding: The minister said no industrial party had reported delays and the Chief Commissioner had not asked for more resources, so no case for extra funding was accepted on the day.

title: HELP FOR SMALL BUSINESS ON IR LAWS
summary: Bevan asked what compliance help small businesses get with state IR laws; the answer pointed to awareness campaigns rather than a dedicated program.
ask: Bevan asked what support or compliance assistance is offered to small businesses unfamiliar with WA industrial relations laws.
answer: The minister said the department runs awareness strategies focused on small-business employers and vulnerable workers, including a Young Workers in WA campaign under the WA Youth Action Plan, and provides information on awards, minimum wages and leave entitlements through its website, the Wageline contact centre and social media.
finding: The government pointed to general awareness campaigns and the Wageline service rather than a dedicated compliance-support program for small businesses new to the state system.

title: $350 MILLION FOR PLANTATIONS, HOW MUCH PLANTED
summary: Bevan sought an itemised account of the Softwood Plantation Expansion Fund and found the land has been bought but the planting figure was taken on notice.
ask: Bevan asked for an itemised account of the $350 million Softwood Plantation Expansion Fund, including how much land in hectares has been purchased, how much has actually been planted, and how much land is forecast to be bought in total from the fund.
answer: Officials said that as at May 2025 the Forest Products Commission had acquired or settled 32 properties totalling more than 10,900 hectares for $115.89 million, and that the account also funds establishment costs such as seedlings, land preparation and plantation management to age two. The figure for how much of that land has actually been planted was taken on notice.
finding: More than $115 million has been spent buying over 10,900 hectares for softwood plantations, but how much of it has actually been planted was not answered on the day and was taken on notice.

title: $161 MILLION FORECAST, NOT REALISED
summary: Bevan pressed on the Ecological Thinning Account receipts and drew an admission that the original targets were optimistic and were missed.
ask: Bevan asked whether the $161 million in receipts forecast for the Ecological Thinning Account in 2024–25 was realised and, if not, how much was, why the 2025–26 forecast is about a fifth of that, and why investment in thinning is not greater given the stated goal of protecting forest health in a drying climate.
answer: Officials confirmed the $161 million forecast was not met, citing a delayed start, markets that were not available, firewood producers already holding stock, and pricing assumptions that were too high. They said the lower 2025–26 figure is a more realistic estimate after an optimistic original target, that there is no backlog of unsold firewood logs because the scale has been matched to the market, and that the scale of thinning is a planning matter for the Department of Biodiversity, Conservation and Attractions.
finding: The government's own forecast for thinning receipts was missed by a wide margin, with officials describing the original targets as optimistic and pointing to a slow start and absent markets.

title: THINNING FLAT, WELL SHORT OF THE PLAN
summary: Bevan probed the Ecological Thinning Account: what the overheads are, who carries them, and why the scale stays flat below the forest management plan target.
ask: Bevan asked what makes up the $8.3 million in overheads inside the $43.1 million forecast drawdown for 2025–26, whether those overheads sit with the Forest Products Commission or the Department of Biodiversity, Conservation and Attractions, and whether flat drawdowns of about $43 million to $47 million across the forward estimates mean thinning will stay well short of the 8,000 hectares set in the forest management plan.
answer: Officials said the overheads are the direct salaries of staff managing the thinning operations plus corporate overheads, with the commission's share in this budget and any departmental share in the department's budget. The minister said the commission carries out thinning on behalf of the Department of Biodiversity, Conservation and Attractions, which plans the activity and informs the numbers, and that costs run through the Treasurer's special purpose account with sale revenue returning to it.
finding: Thinning drawdowns stay flat across the forward estimates, well below the 8,000 hectares in the forest management plan, with the scale set by the department's planning rather than the commission that does the work.

title: SANDALWOOD REVENUE A PLACEHOLDER AFTER 2026
summary: In Forest Products Commission estimates, Bevan tested how forestry revenue is built, and found the out-year sandalwood figure rests on an unsettled licence.
ask: Bevan asked for a breakdown of Forest Products Commission revenue across native forest operations, plantation timber and sandalwood, and, given the sandalwood licence expires on 31 December 2026, what sandalwood revenue is allocated in the out years of the budget.
answer: The minister said revenue is forecast to rise on additional pine sawlog sales, interest and Australian carbon credit units, with uncertainty around softwood prices and sandalwood. Officials said there are no sandalwood volumes beyond 2026 because a new Order in Council is required, so they had used average volumes as a placeholder rather than zero, and that the allowable harvest is set by the Department of Biodiversity, Conservation and Attractions and reviewed in 2026.
finding: The budget's out-year sandalwood revenue is a placeholder, not a forecast, because the licence beyond 2026 has not been settled.

title: AGED CARE AT CAPACITY, FAMILIES MOVED AWAY
summary: As shadow Minister for Aged Care, Bevan said aged-care facilities are at capacity across the state, with elderly residents forced to move away from family, community and support networks to find a place. He said regional providers are scarce, that people approved for a care package often cannot access the staff to deliver it, and that NDIS assessment and funding waits are pushing past 18 months. He said community resource centres have filled the gap through the Staying at Home program but received no additional support in this budget, and that telehealth is no substitute for the face-to-face care older residents need.
quote: Wait times for NDIS assessments and funding are pushing out to 18 months or more, and that is only part of the story.
outcome: His first contribution to the 2025–26 state budget debate (Appropriation (Capital 2025–26) Bill 2025, second reading), foreshadowing the questions he said he would pursue in the following week's budget estimates hearings.

title: FORESTRY SPENDING UP, ACCOUNTABILITY MISSING
summary: As shadow Minister for Forestry, Bevan raised the ecological thinning program. He said budget papers show spending rising from $40.65 million in 2024–25 to $47.13 million by 2028–29 while cash assets stay persistently negative, with no long-term funding strategy, no carbon credit strategy and Treasury appearing to subsidise losses through a special purpose account. He said staffing had risen despite reduced native forest operations, and that the Native Forest Transition program's grant allocation and coupe selections still lack transparency.
quote: The budget papers reveal a consistent year-on-year increase in expenditure for the thinning program, rising from $40.65 million in 2024–25 to $47.13 million by 2028–29, yet there remains no clear evidence that this investment is delivering value for money.
outcome: His first contribution to the 2025–26 state budget debate (Appropriation (Capital 2025–26) Bill 2025, second reading), foreshadowing the questions he said he would pursue in the following week's budget estimates hearings.

title: THE NEEDS LEFT OUT ENTIRELY
summary: Bevan then listed needs left out of the budget entirely: an upgrade for Margaret River Hospital, with no funding allocated to start work; stage 2 of Margaret River Senior High School, still without funding; a promised $1 million reroof of Bridgetown Hospital's leaking emergency department, now nowhere to be seen; funding cuts to Rural West that reduce financial counselling for regional small businesses; and run-down facilities at Boyup Brook District High School and its recreation centre.
quote: Boyup Brook District High School's toilet blocks are run-down, the showers have no doors and there is no hot water.
outcome: His first contribution to the 2025–26 state budget debate (Appropriation (Capital 2025–26) Bill 2025, second reading), foreshadowing the questions he said he would pursue in the following week's budget estimates hearings.

title: $36 MILLION CUT FROM REGIONAL ROADS
summary: Among the needs he said were left out, Bevan singled out the route between Manjimup and Bridgetown, a major freight and tourist road that still has no overtaking lanes. He said that rather than funding them, the budget had removed $36 million from South West regional road funding.
quote: Overtaking lanes are needed between Manjimup and Bridgetown, a major freight and tourist route, yet $36 million has been removed from South West regional road funding in this budget.
outcome: His first contribution to the 2025–26 state budget debate (Appropriation (Capital 2025–26) Bill 2025, second reading), foreshadowing the questions he said he would pursue in the following week's budget estimates hearings.

title: PROMISES MADE, NOT FUNDED
summary: Bevan listed election promises he could not find as funded line items in the 2025–26 budget: $12 million for a new classroom block at Cowaramup Primary School, $400,000 for a car park at Denmark Primary School, $1.25 million for the Manjimup Rea Park and Collier Street redevelopment, and $5 million for a new Margaret River Volunteer Fire and Rescue Service station. He also asked how much of two commitments published in February 2025, support for affordable regional childcare and urgent care clinics for the regions, had been allocated to Warren–Blackwood. He asked the government to honour and fully fund what it had promised.
quote: What we are asking for in Warren–Blackwood is not aspirational; it is essential.
outcome: His first contribution to the 2025–26 state budget debate (Appropriation (Capital 2025–26) Bill 2025, second reading), foreshadowing the questions he said he would pursue in the following week's budget estimates hearings.

title: WHAT THE BUDGET GOT RIGHT, AND ONE CAUTION
summary: Opening his first budget reply as shadow Minister for Forestry and Aged Care, Bevan recognised parts of the budget he welcomed: the Made in WA focus, which he hoped would extend to farmers as well as mining and manufacturing; the push to increase housing, provided service connection wait times are cut in the regions; the telehealth investment; and the ongoing commitment to regional sport and recreation. On telehealth he urged caution, saying it should add to local face-to-face care, not replace it, particularly in emergency departments, hospitals and aged-care services.
quote: Telehealth should complement, not replace, locally based face-to-face health care, particularly in emergency departments, hospitals and aged-care services.
outcome: His first contribution to the 2025–26 state budget debate (Appropriation (Capital 2025–26) Bill 2025, second reading), foreshadowing the questions he said he would pursue in the following week's budget estimates hearings.

title: GEOGRAPHY SHOULD NOT DECIDE A CHILD'S EDUCATION
summary: Bevan tied the regional schools squeeze to a worsening teacher shortage. He cited reports that nearly 4,000 students are taking at least one subject through the School of Isolated and Distance Education, a 42 per cent rise in three years, from 2,824 in 2022 to 3,998 in February 2025, and said this points to a failure to secure and support the teacher workforce in country WA rather than to innovation. He said in-person teaching with a qualified educator is best practice and that too many regional students are missing out.
quote: We cannot let geography determine the quality of a child's education.
outcome: Part of the debate on the opposition's motion condemning the Cook government over its education priorities. Bevan used his contribution to put Margaret River Senior High School stage 2 and the regional teacher shortage on the parliamentary record.

title: $36 MILLION TAKEN OUT OF PUBLIC EDUCATION
summary: Bevan said the government had moved to dissolve the Public Education Endowment Trust and redirect nearly $36 million into consolidated revenue with no requirement it ever be spent on education again. He said the opposition supported repealing the outdated trust model, but argued the money should be quarantined and directed into public education where the need is greatest, such as Margaret River.
quote: Meanwhile, the Cook government has moved to dissolve the Public Education Endowment Trust that was created to support schools and redirect nearly $36 million into consolidated revenue, with no requirement it will ever be spent on education again.
outcome: Part of the debate on the opposition's motion condemning the Cook government over its education priorities. Bevan used his contribution to put Margaret River Senior High School stage 2 and the regional teacher shortage on the parliamentary record.

title: THE SCHOOLS LEFT OFF THE LIST
summary: Beyond Margaret River, Bevan said the same pattern runs across Warren–Blackwood: Cowaramup Primary School struggling with growth, Margaret River Primary School under similar pressure with no investment on the horizon, Boyup Brook District High School needing upgrades, and Manjimup Senior High School overlooked again in this budget.
quote: Cowaramup Primary School is struggling with growth and facilities that are not keeping up.
outcome: Part of the debate on the opposition's motion condemning the Cook government over its education priorities. Bevan used his contribution to put Margaret River Senior High School stage 2 and the regional teacher shortage on the parliamentary record.

title: MILLIONS FOR BURSWOOD, NOTHING FOR THE SCHOOL
summary: Bevan set the budget's priorities side by side: billions flowing to Metronet and the Burswood entertainment precinct, including $217 million for a racetrack and entertainment precinct and $60 million for a rugby deal, while upgrades for regional schools were left out. He said the message to the regions was clear, that if you are not in Perth you are not a priority.
quote: How do we justify $217 million for a brand new racetrack and entertainment precinct in Burswood and $60 million for a rugby deal while doing nothing for a school in one of state's fastest growing regions?
outcome: Part of the debate on the opposition's motion condemning the Cook government over its education priorities. Bevan used his contribution to put Margaret River Senior High School stage 2 and the regional teacher shortage on the parliamentary record.

title: STAGE 2 MISSING FROM THE BUDGET AGAIN
summary: Speaking to the opposition's motion on the government's education priorities, Bevan said Margaret River Senior High School, built for 650 and now over 1,200 with projections beyond 2,000, is out of space, with every classroom in use every period, the staffroom turned into a classroom, and programs such as home economics, dance, woodwork, textiles and farm mechanics run from transportables. He said the school board, chaired by Barbara Maidment, has made repeated formal requests and asked the new Minister for Education to visit, with no visit to date, and that he had raised the issue in Parliament on at least three occasions, yet stage 2 was again absent from the budget.
quote: Margaret River Senior High School has been completely missed as a line item in this year's budget.
outcome: Part of the debate on the opposition's motion condemning the Cook government over its education priorities. Bevan used his contribution to put Margaret River Senior High School stage 2 and the regional teacher shortage on the parliamentary record.

title: RELEASE THE MARGARET RIVER HOSPITAL BUSINESS CASE
summary: Bevan asked the Minister for Health when the government would give Margaret River Hospital the capacity to care for a community and visitor numbers that have stretched it beyond capacity for years, in one of the fastest growing regions in the state. The minister replied by pointing to a $3.2 billion health infrastructure budget, telehealth, regional staffing incentives and an increase in the Patient Assisted Travel Scheme rate from 26c to 40c a kilometre, and said the government was always assessing its infrastructure needs, without committing to the hospital. In a supplementary question Bevan asked specifically when the minister would release the business case for the upgrade.
quote: When will the minister release the business case for the desperately required upgrades to Margaret River Hospital?
outcome: The minister did not commit to the upgrade or to releasing the business case, saying she would talk to the Minister for Health Infrastructure about whether it is proposed to release it.

title: THE PEOPLE CLOSEST TO THE LAND
summary: Bevan used a member's statement to thank the Warren–Blackwood locals who helped his office review firearms legislation and forest policy over recent months. He said the people closest to the land, those who work it, care for it and defend it, are often the most knowledgeable and the least heard, and he wanted their contribution on the record. He named Gavin Butcher for decades of forestry experience, John Clarke and the Bushfire Front for keeping prescribed burning and fire readiness on the agenda, and the Denmark Pistol Club, Manjimup Pistol Club, Manjimup Rifle Club and the Sporting Shooters' Association of Australia for practical input on firearm reform. He also thanked volunteer pest controller Anton Smith and wildlife carer Sasha Boundy, whose work he said shows that conservation and primary industries are partners rather than enemies in managing the landscape.
quote: It is these community contributors who help shape balanced, commonsense policy.
outcome: A member's statement recognising the community members across Warren–Blackwood who contributed to his office's review of firearms and forest policy.

title: MANJIMUP, THE OFF-ROAD CAPITAL OF WA
summary: Bevan spoke in support of the Control of Vehicles (Off-road Areas) Amendment Bill 2025, using his home town of Manjimup, where 83% of the shire is still forest, to make the case for safe, well-managed off-road riding. He traced the Manjimup trail bike hub from a $15,000 shire feasibility study in 2015 to a $980,000 Regional Recovery Partnership grant in 2021 for stages 1 to 3, built on forest access tracks no longer used for logging and developed closely with the Department of Biodiversity, Conservation and Attractions. He said riders are encouraged to register so their route is known if something goes wrong, and that the hub is projected to draw around 33,000 visitors and inject about $12.8 million a year once complete. He said he would support whatever licensing approach is decided and commended the bill to the house.
quote: It is probably the off-road capital of Western Australia, if not the world.
outcome: A contribution in support of the Control of Vehicles (Off-road Areas) Amendment Bill 2025, spotlighting the Manjimup trail bike hub as a regional tourism opportunity built on former forestry tracks.

title: GROWTH TOWNS OUTRUNNING THEIR SERVICES
summary: Bevan closed on the growing towns of Cowaramup and Denmark, which he said are expanding rapidly without the roads, waste systems, mains water, sporting facilities and digital connectivity to match. He said towns that were once small are now hubs for young families and tourists, bringing traffic congestion, limited parking, childcare waitlists and pressure on schools, doctors and community services. He used Cowaramup as the example of where planning, policy and infrastructure delivery has not kept pace with reality.
quote: Cowaramup is a perfect example of where planning, policy and infrastructure delivery simply has not kept pace with reality.
outcome: Part of the debate on the motion that the house condemn the Cook government for its failure to deliver core services and infrastructure in regional Western Australia. Bevan used his contribution to put a detailed list of Warren–Blackwood service gaps on the parliamentary record.

title: NOT ENOUGH SAFE HOUSING IN THE REGIONS
summary: Bevan raised the shortage of women's shelters and crisis accommodation across Warren–Blackwood. He said family and domestic violence does not stop at the city limits, and that it can be harder for victims to leave in small towns where anonymity is impossible and support services are far away. He said frontline workers are being forced to turn people away for lack of space, funding and case management support, and asked the government to prioritise safe housing for women and children in regional WA. Support is available: 1800RESPECT on 1800 737 732, or 000 in an emergency.
quote: Family and domestic violence does not stop at the city limits.
outcome: Part of the debate on the motion that the house condemn the Cook government for its failure to deliver core services and infrastructure in regional Western Australia. Bevan used his contribution to put a detailed list of Warren–Blackwood service gaps on the parliamentary record.

title: MOVING AWAY FROM FAMILY TO FIND A BED
summary: Bevan said regional aged care is under pressure on bed numbers, staffing, respite services and allied health support, with more families struggling to care for ageing parents using local resources. He said families are being forced to move elderly parents to another town simply to find a place, away from their support networks. He called that outcome unacceptable for everyone involved.
quote: Having to move to another town just to find a bed in an aged-care facility is unacceptable.
outcome: Part of the debate on the motion that the house condemn the Cook government for its failure to deliver core services and infrastructure in regional Western Australia. Bevan used his contribution to put a detailed list of Warren–Blackwood service gaps on the parliamentary record.

title: NO CT SCANNER AT MANJIMUP HOSPITAL
summary: Bevan raised the absence of a CT scanner at Manjimup Hospital, a service he said is taken for granted in the city. Without it, patients who need urgent imaging are transported hours away, delaying diagnosis and adding strain on patients, carers, volunteers and emergency services. For a growing community with a significantly older population, he said that is unacceptable.
quote: At Manjimup Hospital, there is no CT scanner, a service that is taken for granted in the city.
outcome: Part of the debate on the motion that the house condemn the Cook government for its failure to deliver core services and infrastructure in regional Western Australia. Bevan used his contribution to put a detailed list of Warren–Blackwood service gaps on the parliamentary record.

title: AN $80 MILLION FUND WITH NO PUBLIC CLARITY
summary: Bevan pressed the government on the $80 million native forest transition fund, announced to support the workers, businesses and communities affected by the end of native forestry. He said operators and businesses who are eligible had been given no contact and had received no compensation, and that there had been little public clarity on the criteria, timeframes and outcomes of grant decisions. He framed it as a governance question as much as a forestry one, asking how decisions are being made, who is receiving funds and how equity is being maintained across the transition.
quote: There has been little public clarity on the criteria, timeframes and outcomes of grant decisions under this program.
outcome: Part of the debate on the motion that the house condemn the Cook government for its failure to deliver core services and infrastructure in regional Western Australia. Bevan used his contribution to put a detailed list of Warren–Blackwood service gaps on the parliamentary record.

title: A FREIGHT HIGHWAY WITH NO PASSING LANES
summary: Bevan turned to the South Western Highway between Bridgetown and Manjimup, a freight and tourism route used daily by school buses, heavy trucks, caravans and locals. He said the lack of passing lanes leaves drivers stuck behind slow traffic for 20, 30 or sometimes 40 kilometres, which he described as both dangerous and inefficient for one of the state's key agricultural zones. His point was that overtaking infrastructure is a question of safety and productivity, not convenience.
quote: Drivers are stuck behind slow-moving traffic for 20, 30 or sometimes 40 kilometres, which is dangerous and inefficient.
outcome: Part of the debate on the motion that the house condemn the Cook government for its failure to deliver core services and infrastructure in regional Western Australia. Bevan used his contribution to put a detailed list of Warren–Blackwood service gaps on the parliamentary record.

title: BUILT FOR 650, NOW TEACHING MORE THAN 1,200
summary: Speaking to the motion on regional services, Bevan set out the overcrowding at Margaret River Senior High School. A campus built for 650 students now holds more than 1,200, with teachers giving up their staffroom and specialist programs such as home economics, woodwork and dance running out of transportables. He said the school has no spare rooms and nowhere to put more transportables, yet stage 2 of the expansion, for which funding was committed under the previous Liberal–National government in 2016–17, still has no commitment to start.
quote: Built to serve 650 students, the school is now bursting at the seams with more than 1,200 enrolled students.
outcome: Part of the debate on the motion that the house condemn the Cook government for its failure to deliver core services and infrastructure in regional Western Australia. Bevan used his contribution to put a detailed list of Warren–Blackwood service gaps on the parliamentary record.

title: NO MODELLING ON SALVAGE TIMBER VALUE
summary: Bevan asked whether the government had conducted any economic modelling comparing the value of salvage logs used as sawlogs against their use for woodchips or charcoal.
quote: Has the government conducted any economic modelling on the comparative value of salvage logs?
outcome: The Parliamentary Secretary did not provide any modelling, saying the question should be directed to the forestry portfolio.

title: HOW SALVAGE TIMBER IS ALLOCATED
summary: Bevan asked what process decides where salvage timber is sent and how it is used, and whether timber is allocated through public tender auction or by private agreement. Where allocation is by private agreement, he asked what checks are in place to ensure transparency and fairness.
quote: What checks are in place to ensure transparency and fairness?
outcome: The Parliamentary Secretary directed both questions to the Minister for Forestry, saying allocation and any tender or private arrangements fall outside the bill and sit with the forestry portfolio.

title: WHO CAN APPROVE SALVAGE REMOVAL
summary: Bevan asked who has the authority to approve the removal of salvage forest products from state forests and timber reserves, and whether that sits with the Department of Biodiversity, Conservation and Attractions, the Forest Products Commission or another entity. He confirmed he was asking about commercial removal.
quote: Who has the authority to approve the removal of salvage forest products from state forests or timber reserves?
outcome: The Parliamentary Secretary said clause 81 of the Conservation and Land Management Regulations enables removal of salvage products in prescribed circumstances, and that the decision on what is removed rests with the Forest Products Commission.

title: HOW SALVAGE TIMBER IS DEFINED
summary: Bevan asked what guidelines or documented criteria are used to define salvage forest product, whether those criteria are publicly available, and whether they are reviewed by an independent body.
quote: What guidelines or documented criteria are used to define salvage forest product?
