outcome: The Parliamentary Secretary said the Forest Management Plan 2024–33 is the overarching guiding document for managing the forest, including the removal of any salvage products.

title: THE FUTURE OF CERTIFIED TIMBER
summary: Bevan noted that timber from Western Australia's native forests is certified as sustainably managed, which allows it to be used for high-value purposes such as furniture and flooring, and asked whether the legislation would let that certification and practice continue. He also pointed to large stockpiles of salvage forest product building up at mills including Gelorup, Deanmill and Diamond, the result of restrictions on the contracts the Forest Products Commission is able to enter into under the forest management plan.
quote: Will the legislation enable this certification and practice to continue?
outcome: The Parliamentary Secretary directed the certification question to the Minister for Forestry. On the stockpiles, she said the Department of Biodiversity, Conservation and Attractions has no remit and that any commercial use of salvage product is a matter for the Forest Products Commission.

title: STILL NO STAGE 2 START DATE
summary: Bevan asked the Minister for Education when the Margaret River Senior High School community would receive an update on the start of stage 2, noting the school was built for 650 students, now holds more than 1,200 and is projected to exceed 2,000 in the coming years. In a supplementary question he asked when the minister would commit to visiting the school, after she had previously declined an invitation citing schedule constraints. The minister spoke about statewide education spending and her record of school visits, but gave no date for a stage 2 update and made no commitment to visit.
quote: After previously declining an invitation to visit the school, citing schedule constraints, when will the minister commit to visiting Margaret River Senior High School to see the situation firsthand?
outcome: The minister did not give a date for a stage 2 update or commit to visiting the school, instead pointing to statewide education spending and saying she visits many schools and would be in the Wheatbelt that Friday.

title: CHEMICAL THINNING WITH NO REPORTING
summary: Bevan raised the use of chemical thinning with glyphosate across native forests that also serve as water catchments, with no long-term study of the impact, no examination of transmission into domestic water supplies, and no public reporting framework. He said this is chemical management of a system once managed through harvesting and controlled burns, and that the bill sets up no glyphosate reporting framework at all.
quote: There is no transparency and no public reporting, just quiet application of a potentially harmful substance to some of our state's most sensitive natural areas.
outcome: Despite the opposition's objections, the Conservation and Land Management Amendment Bill 2025 passed and received royal assent on 24 June 2025.

title: SPECIALTY TIMBER LOCKED AWAY
summary: Bevan said ending access to specialty native timbers like jarrah, marri and sheoak cuts off the artisans and craftspeople who make furniture, musical instruments, heritage restoration and artworks from them. Pointing to the jarrah panelling of the chamber itself, harvested by generations of South West foresters and millers, he said the bill offers no commitment to keep specialty timber available.
quote: The closure of access to special new timbers does not just impact industry; it severs the hands of artists who have spent decades working with this material.
outcome: Despite the opposition's objections, the Conservation and Land Management Amendment Bill 2025 passed and received royal assent on 24 June 2025.

title: IMPORTED TIMBER BRINGS BIOSECURITY RISK
summary: Bevan warned that replacing local timber with imports carries a biosecurity risk, raising the polyphagous shot-hole borer as a threat to species like marri, banksia and paperbark and to the avocado industry. He said WA already has the lowest tree canopy of any capital city after borer losses in places like Kings Park, and that swapping a sustainable local industry for an opaque global supply chain adds freight emissions on top of the risk.
quote: We swapped local management of a sustainable industry for an opaque global supply chain, and we added freight emissions on top of it.
outcome: Despite the opposition's objections, the Conservation and Land Management Amendment Bill 2025 passed and received royal assent on 24 June 2025.

title: LOCAL FIREWOOD OPERATORS PRICED OUT
summary: Bevan said the firewood problem after the native forest ban is one of quality and fairness, not quantity. He told the house premium jarrah is being funnelled to a single foreign-owned charcoal company through a non-competitive negotiated agreement at a preferential rate, while local firewood operators compete for lower-grade marri and karri carted from distant sites and sold at higher cost. He noted the Forest Products Commission's own list of firewood businesses was last updated in 2022 and includes operators that have since closed.
quote: The government's approach has prioritised deals with big business over the survival of local small firewood suppliers.
outcome: Despite the opposition's objections, the Conservation and Land Management Amendment Bill 2025 passed and received royal assent on 24 June 2025.

title: FOREST CLOSURE WEAKENED BUSHFIRE RESPONSE
summary: Bevan argued the closure stripped the South West of bushfire-fighting capacity, not just trees. He said the trained timber crews and specialised machinery, the bulldozers, excavators and operators who once worked multi-day fire events alongside the Department of Biodiversity, Conservation and Attractions, have largely left the region or moved to mining, and that private earthmoving contractors who filled emergency roles have sold their gear as demand vanished.
quote: We have not just lost trees; we have lost the operational infrastructure that kept our forests and communities safe from bushfires.
outcome: Despite the opposition's objections, the Conservation and Land Management Amendment Bill 2025 passed and received royal assent on 24 June 2025.

title: FOREST CLOSURE COST JOBS AND TRUST
summary: Speaking against the Conservation and Land Management Amendment Bill 2025, Bevan said the end of native forest logging has cost the South West hundreds of jobs, small businesses and a sustainable local timber supply, along with the intergenerational knowledge that went with them. He noted the Forest Products Commission held internationally recognised certification for sustainable native forest management until 29 June 2025, and that when the government was asked to table peer-reviewed science justifying the shutdown, none was produced.
quote: This closure does not just mark a policy change; it marks a loss of identity, the loss of hundreds of regional jobs, the loss of small businesses, the loss of a sustainable supply of high-quality timber for our homes.
outcome: Despite the opposition's objections, the Conservation and Land Management Amendment Bill 2025 passed and received royal assent on 24 June 2025.

title: REGIONAL SCHOOLS NEED BASIC FACILITIES
summary: Bevan listed schools across the electorate needing basic facilities: Manjimup Senior High School delivering student services from a front office that lacks privacy, Cowaramup Primary School running out of space as young families move in, Margaret River Primary School facing the same limits as the high school, Bridgetown Primary School with no hot water in its toilets, and Boyup Brook District High School needing upgrades for its rural students. He argued regional schools deserve the same planning and funding as their metropolitan counterparts.
quote: These are not optional extras; they are essential services for growing regional communities, and they deserve the same level of planning, support and funding as our metropolitan counterparts.
outcome: A contribution to the debate on the Public Education Endowment Repeal Bill 2025, arguing the wound-up fund's $36 million should be quarantined for public education and pointing to overcrowding at Margaret River Senior High School.

title: KEEP $36 MILLION IN PUBLIC SCHOOLS
summary: Bevan said the opposition agreed the Public Education Endowment is outdated, but argued that winding it up should not mean moving nearly $36 million intended for public education into consolidated revenue. He called for the money to be placed in a dedicated public education infrastructure fund and spent on the schools that need it most, rather than disappearing into general revenue.
quote: That money was meant for our kids, our classrooms and our schools.
outcome: A contribution to the debate on the Public Education Endowment Repeal Bill 2025, arguing the wound-up fund's $36 million should be quarantined for public education and pointing to overcrowding at Margaret River Senior High School.

title: MARGARET RIVER HIGH SCHOOL OUT OF SPACE
summary: Bevan told the house that Margaret River Senior High School, built for 650 students, now has 1,220 enrolled and is forecast to pass 2,000 within a few years, yet is already out of space. Every room is in use all day, the staffroom has been turned into a classroom, and home economics, woodwork, dance, drama, textiles and farm mechanics are being run out of transportables, with metalwork and other specialist learning due to follow. He noted the school paid more than $40,000 in classroom rental last year, and that there is still no funding commitment for stage 2 of its expansion.
quote: This school is so short of classroom space that the teachers have given up their space to accommodate the kids.
outcome: A contribution to the debate on the Public Education Endowment Repeal Bill 2025, arguing the wound-up fund's $36 million should be quarantined for public education and pointing to overcrowding at Margaret River Senior High School.

title: THE VOLUNTEERS WHO RUN OUR REGION
summary: During National Volunteer Week, Bevan recognised the volunteers who keep Warren–Blackwood running, from paramedics and firefighters to wildlife carers, SES teams and aged-care support. He singled out Jamie Copeland, captain of the Pemberton Volunteer Fire and Rescue Service and a finalist in the 2025 WA Fire and Emergency Services Awards for his leadership and his work mentoring young people through the Youth Emergency Service cadets, and congratulated the Northcliffe Volunteer Fire and Rescue Service and the Northcliffe Bush Fire Brigade, also named finalists. He opened by congratulating Manjimup's Marty Zambotto, known as Go-Jo, who represented Australia at Eurovision.
quote: In our region it is not just nice to have volunteers, it is essential.
outcome: A National Volunteer Week member's statement recognising the emergency-services, health and care volunteers across Warren–Blackwood, and congratulating local award finalists.

title: HEALTH CHECKS COULD DETER HELP
summary: Bevan said the compliance task is causing real mental strain for law-abiding owners trying to meet difficult requirements in a short timeframe, and that a growing number of GPs are refusing or reluctant to complete the required physical and mental-health checks for fear of the consequences to themselves, which further delays or denies people their lawful right to a licence.
quote: Mental health does not just deteriorate overnight. It is worn down day by day through anxiety, confusion, fear of breaking the law and the weight of being portrayed as a danger to society for simply owning a tool that we have used safely for decades.
outcome: The disallowance motion was defeated 13 votes to 37, so the Firearms Regulations 2024 remain in force.

title: PEST CONTROL KEEPS WILDLIFE IN BALANCE
summary: Bevan warned the regulations are driving professional and recreational shooters out of pest control, with consequences for wildlife and road safety. He said unmanaged kangaroo numbers mean more vehicle collisions on regional roads and more crop damage, that police are increasingly called out to euthanise injured animals, and that feral cats, foxes, rabbits and pigs go less controlled. Citing the federal threatened species strategy finding that feral cats alone kill more than 1.5 billion native animals a year, he argued that stifling practical pest control puts native species at greater risk.
quote: This is about giving the people who care for the land the tools they need to do so properly.
outcome: The disallowance motion was defeated 13 votes to 37, so the Firearms Regulations 2024 remain in force.

title: FARMERS NEED TO END ANIMAL SUFFERING
summary: Bevan set out the everyday reality of humane euthanasia on the land: a kangaroo hit at dusk, livestock lost in a bushfire, a horse with a broken leg hours from a vet, and a constituent handing in the .22 he uses to end a dying lamb's suffering because the new system is too hard to navigate. He described having to put down a cow with eye cancer he had cared for over a decade, then noted that because his property is 700 acres rather than the 2,000 the rules require, he would no longer be eligible to hold a rifle of the calibre needed to euthanise large livestock humanely.
quote: This is not about privilege; this is about animal welfare. This is about the mental health of the people looking after these animals.
outcome: The disallowance motion was defeated 13 votes to 37, so the Firearms Regulations 2024 remain in force.

title: FIREARM RULES UNWORKABLE FOR REGIONAL WA
summary: Bevan, a licensed firearm owner, said he backed the goal of community safety but that the Firearms Regulations 2024 are unworkable for the regional people who use firearms as everyday tools. He pointed to an online portal that does not work, poor connectivity, and compliant gun cabinets and security adding up to more than $10,000 per owner, alongside a medical-clearance step that many GPs are declining to perform. He cited Australian Institute of Criminology figures that only 9.4 per cent of firearm homicides involved a gun registered to the offender, arguing the rules burden law-abiding owners while doing little about the illicit guns behind most firearm crime.
quote: It looks tough, it sounds reassuring, but in practice it misses the mark.
outcome: The disallowance motion was defeated 13 votes to 37, so the Firearms Regulations 2024 remain in force.

title: KEEP YOUNG PEOPLE CLOSE TO HOME
summary: Bevan said some schools in the electorate are past capacity and young people are leaving because they cannot see a future at home. He called for expanded training, better digital infrastructure and real local jobs so young people can build a future in their home towns rather than treating a move to the city as their only option.
quote: Moving to the city should not be the only option that they see as an opportunity.
outcome: His maiden speech to the Legislative Assembly, naming youth retention, training and local jobs as a priority.

title: BETTER REGIONAL HEALTH AND AGED CARE
summary: With one in seven residents aged over 65, above the state average, Bevan said Warren–Blackwood needs better access to GPs, allied health and aged care. He committed to advocating for regional health initiatives that work for an ageing community.
quote: With a high proportion of older residents than the state average, we need better access to GPs, allied health and aged care.
outcome: His maiden speech to the Legislative Assembly, naming regional health and aged care as a priority.

title: FAIR LAND USE AND PROPERTY RIGHTS
summary: Bevan raised property rights, saying government overreach has left home owners, landowners and farmers being told what they can and cannot do on land that some have managed for generations. He said the balance has tipped too far and committed to land-use laws that respect rural knowledge and experience.
quote: I will fight for fair land use laws and personal freedoms that respect rural knowledge and experience.
outcome: His maiden speech to the Legislative Assembly, naming fair land use and property rights as a priority.

title: KEEP FAMILY FARMS VIABLE
summary: Bevan said climate change is already affecting regional WA and that the response should be practical, not ideological. He called for drought-resilience measures, better soil health and support that keeps family farms viable, arguing that food production matters to city and country alike.
quote: We need practical policies to build drought resilience, improve soil health and keep family farms viable.
outcome: His maiden speech to the Legislative Assembly, naming drought resilience and farm viability as a priority.

title: FORESTS CANNOT BE LOCKED AND LEFT
summary: Bevan argued that ending native forestry without active management leaves the South West's forests vulnerable. He said decades of human interference, introduced weeds and pests and over-clearing mean the forests cannot simply be locked up and walked away from, and that without investment in forest health the bushfire risk grows. He put it plainly: act now, or risk lives and property to a preventable fire later.
quote: If we do not act now, forest health will decline and turn into a tinderbox.
outcome: His maiden speech to the Legislative Assembly, naming active forest management and bushfire prevention as a priority.

title: WATER SECURITY FOR A DRYING REGION
summary: As a former chair of the WA Water Users Coalition, Bevan argued water policy should be practical rather than political. He said the region's irrigation districts are vital to food production and that, as the climate dries, the state needs to secure supply and invest in smarter water systems to protect both farming and the environment.
quote: We must secure supply, invest in smarter systems and protect our food-producing regions and environment as we face a drying climate.
outcome: His maiden speech to the Legislative Assembly, naming water and food security as a priority for the electorate.

title: FIRST WORDS FOR WARREN–BLACKWOOD
summary: Bevan opened his time in Parliament by introducing himself as a third-generation Manjimup farmer, business owner and father of two, representing an electorate of more than 43,000 people across six shires of forests, farms and coast. He set out the three principles he intends to work by, local values, trusted leadership and unwavering advocacy, and said he came to build bridges between the city and the country rather than to score political points.
quote: Regional people should never be second-class citizens just because we live outside the city.
outcome: His maiden speech to the Legislative Assembly, framing the values and priorities he intends to pursue across the 42nd Parliament.
